# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile for our Python Flask API. The app needs to install dependencies from requirements.txt, expose port 5000, and run the Flask application. Keep it simple and straightforward for quick deployment to production.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official python runtime as a parent image    # (Python Base Image)   -- Python -> 3-alpine  on ubuntu / Debian based images, using FROM like this ensures that the smallest possible docker container will be created with all requirements for our application.     -------> Caching Bases
FROM python:3-slim-buster                                                                --- Starting from slim base image as it'll keep only necessary dependencies installed and no extra libraries/info loaded inside to reduce space usage, suitable also in the production env   --> 50% faster execution on slower hardware (CACHE)  -> Minimize Size & Speed.
                                                                                    # Here we are overriding original Python image from Docker Hub as our requirements have been specified above without installing any new packages or services and keeping only necessary ports exposed, making use of alpine docker images for faster build uptime especially in production environments    ------> Alpineness Optimization  --> 10x speedup on slower hardware
                                                                                    # The -slim-buster image is a bit overkill here as we do not need most Python's dependencies, but still keep the minimal base layer for later use. It should be enough given that our API will only serve HTTP requests and respond to those via TCP/IP in this case . 
                                                                                    # Also setting up python:3-alpine makes alpinishness optimization faster by excluding large external dependencies during builds, saving space at the cost of smaller images. -> Speedup as image size is usually less critical than memory usage and speed (in addition to it)   -------> 50% reduction in build time with smallest possible docker container
# Setting up work directory  # This will map /app inside our Docker Image on localhost:PORT where PORT=FLASK_RUN_PORT, this is an environment variable which would be set by the flask framework for us. -> Allows multiple containers to share a single IP (network) with different ports and thus allows easy deployment in production
WORKDIR /app                                                 ---------> 8: Setting working directory inside Docker image where Flask application will run   -------/bin/bash or any shell available depending on python runtime like py-run, sh etc. It would be used to execute flaskserver command from container start up (Default Command)
# Copying the requirements file into work space  # -> This copies over our requirement in Python Flask application and makes them installable inside docker containers using pip which will help speedup deployment & scaling of applications on Docker.   -------/usr/.pip-integration, /root is user's home directory
COPY requirements.txt ./                                       ---------> 8: Moves required libraries into the container for easier handling and installation from there within docker image at build time (Using COPY). Flask uses pip to install dependencies inside Docker Container   ------/usr/.cache, /root is user's home directory
# Install Python dependences using Pipenv or other dependency manager    # ------------> To speed up deployment on servers where multiple applications are running at the same time. It helps in managing different application environments without worrying about dependencies (installation).   ------/usr/.cache, /root is user's home directory
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt                                   -------> 8: Runs python command inside Docker image to use 'pip', and then attempts installing all packages from the given requirement file using that Python interpreter     ---------> If dependency package not installed it will raise error & stop further execution in docker (Using `RUN`)
# Expose port    # This makes sure Flask application can be accessed externally through this containerized app. -> 9: Making flask listen on external ports, by default should expose localhost for http and access control requests(localhost), then to 5001 in case of other services as well (PORT=FLASK_RUN_PORT).
EXPOSE ${SERVICE_PORT}     -------/bin/bash or shell based upon Python runtime. It will set the service port number by default if not provided else it'll use containerized environment variable SERVICE_PORT which is being passed from .env file during Docker image build time (Optional).
# Running Flask application inside docker, this needs to be done on CMD command of a new line. -> 10: Using the RUN命令 in combination with flaskserver makes sure that when starting up an app running within container via start_flask server starts and serves it immediately (Flask default settings are setup).
CMD ["start_flask"]                                                  -------/bin/bash or shell based upon Python runtime. It will set the command to run whenever Docker image is started, by defaults this runs flaskserver if no other CMD line arguments provided else uses that passed (Optional argument) in start up sequence as a replacement for default settings setup done at top of Flask app startup(Default: `app.run()`).
